fuzzy_prd:
We need a command-line tool that helps developers manage multiple versions of an external SDK side by side. The tool should accept version strings in whatever natural form developers typically type them and automatically normalize each one to the exact identifier used by the upstream release catalog — handle this the same way as the version resolution module does when it tries an exact match first, then tries a slightly modified form. The tool must be able to query the upstream catalog and report, for each queried identifier, whether it is published there or not; results should be emitted in the same order the references were provided. For locally cached versions, the tool needs to verify integrity: a cached version is only healthy if two specific sub-folders are both present; if one or both are missing, the tool should print a human-readable cleanup notice (naming the problematic version) before reporting it as not correctly set up. When fetching a new version from the upstream repository and the reference doesn't exist, the tool must report a structured, language-neutral error without exposing any internal exception details. Installation must reject a missing version reference upfront with a distinct error code rather than attempting a download. Finally, the tool should report its own software version number on demand. All output lines must follow a consistent key=value format terminated by a newline, and error output must always include a second line echoing back the rejected reference — handle error formatting consistently with the fetch failure module.

One extra bit on version lookup since folks asked: bare numeric refs like '1.8.0' should be treated the same way inferFlutterVersion() in golden/lib/utils/helpers.dart does it. It first checks if the version string (lowercased) appears verbatim in the upstream SDK list returned by flutterListAllSdks(). If that is not found, it prepends 'v' and checks again, so something like '1.8.0' can end up resolving to 'v1.8.0'. If the published thing is already a dev or pre-release tag without the prefix, like '1.17.0-dev.3.1', it stays exactly as-is. Channel names 'master', 'stable', 'dev', 'beta' short-circuit early via isFlutterChannel() and always resolve to themselves. Resolution is case-insensitive, and if neither form matches it throws ExceptionNotValidVersion.

Also, for local cache verification, if a version directory exists but is missing either the '.github' folder or the 'bin' folder, or both, the tool should print exactly '<version> exists but was not setup correctly. Doing cleanup...' followed by a newline, then remove the directory, then emit 'installed_correctly=false\n'. That cleanup notice should use the exact version string provided as input. If the directory does not exist at all, there is no cleanup notice in that case, only 'installed_correctly=false\n'.

On the fetch failure path, when fetch_version fails because the reference does not exist upstream, the output is exactly two lines: first 'error=could_not_clone\n', then 'version=<the requested reference>\n'. The requested reference needs to be echoed back verbatim, including exact case, and we still do not include any exception details or host-runtime info.

And for the tool’s own version output, the tool_version action prints exactly '0.8.2' followed by a newline, with no other output. That value comes from the packageVersion constant defined in the generated version source file.

trigger_keywords: what are the two lines output when a version reference is invalid
what is the error format for resolve_version failure
how is an invalid_version error reported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
When resolve_version cannot match the reference (neither as-given nor with a 'v' prefix) in the upstream catalog, the output is exactly two lines: first 'error=invalid_version
', then 'version=<the rejected reference>
'. The reference is echoed back verbatim. A successful resolution emits a single line: 'reference=<canonical identifier>
'.

trigger_keywords: what is the error when install is called without a version
what does install output when no version reference is provided
how does missing version during install get reported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the install action is invoked with no version argument and no project-level pinned version is configured, the tool outputs exactly one line: 'error=missing_version\n'. Only this single line is produced — there is no second 'version=' line for this error code.

trigger_keywords: which two folders must be present for a version to be installed correctly
what sub-directories are required in a healthy cached version directory
what makes a cached version directory valid
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A cached version directory is considered correctly installed only when BOTH of the following sub-directories exist inside it: (1) '.github' (the upstream project-metadata folder) and (2) 'bin' (the executables folder). If the version directory itself does not exist, or if either '.github' or 'bin' is missing, the version is not correctly installed. The output key is 'installed_correctly' with value 'true' or 'false'.

trigger_keywords: what are the four valid release channel names
which channel identifiers are recognized by the tool
what moving channel names does the manager support
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The four recognized release channel names are exactly: 'master', 'stable', 'dev', and 'beta'. These are matched case-insensitively (the version string is lowercased before comparison). Channel names always resolve to themselves without any 'v' prefix and are never prefixed even if a 'v'-prefixed form exists in the catalog.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: handle error formatting consistently with the fetch failure module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Refers to the _fetchVersion() handler in bin/rcb_dispatcher.dart. On failure it emits 'error=<category>\n' on the first line and 'version=<ref>\n' on the second line. The same two-line pattern is used by _resolveVersion() for invalid_version errors. The error category and reference are always on separate newline-terminated lines.

oracle_data.fallback_response: The requested behavior is not explicitly covered by the documented constraints. Please check the dispatcher logic in bin/rcb_dispatcher.dart and the core helpers in golden/lib/utils/helpers.dart and golden/lib/utils/flutter_tools.dart for the relevant implementation details.

feature1_resolve_version.json.description: Normalize a user-supplied SDK version reference into the canonical identifier used by the manager. The input is matched case-insensitively. A bare release number that is published only under a leading-'v' tag is resolved to that prefixed tag; a reference that is already a known tag (with or without the 'v' prefix, including pre-release/development tags that are published without a prefix) is returned unchanged. A reference that matches no known release tag in either its given or 'v'-prefixed form is rejected as an invalid version, and the rejected reference is echoed back in a neutral error.

feature2_release_catalog.json.description: Query the catalog of all SDK versions published by the upstream source and report, for each reference asked about, whether it appears in that catalog. The input supplies a list of references to look up; for every reference the manager emits a line stating whether it is present in or absent from the published catalog, preserving the order of the queried references.

feature3_install_check.json.description: Judge whether a locally cached SDK version directory is a healthy, fully set-up installation. A cached version is considered correctly installed only when its directory exists and contains both the upstream project-metadata folder and the executables (bin) folder. If the directory does not exist, it is simply reported as not correctly installed. If the directory exists but is missing either required marker folder, it is treated as a corrupted partial install: a notice naming the version is printed, the broken directory is cleaned up (removed), and it is reported as not correctly installed. The input describes the version's name and the layout of its cached directory (whether it exists, whether the metadata folder is present, whether the bin folder is present).

cases.expected_output:
v2.0.0 exists but was not setup correctly. Doing cleanup...
installed_correctly=false

feature4_fetch_unknown.json.description: Attempt to fetch (clone) an SDK version reference from the upstream source repository. When the requested reference does not correspond to any real branch or tag upstream, the fetch fails and the manager reports a neutral could-not-clone error that echoes back the requested reference rather than leaking any host-runtime fetch details.

feature5_install_requires_version.json.description: Invoke the install action without supplying any version reference and with no project-level pinned version configured. With nothing to resolve, the install action must refuse to proceed and surface a neutral missing-version error instead of attempting any download.

feature6_tool_version.json.description: Invoke the version action of the command-line tool, which prints the manager's own semantic version string on a single line.
